title: Your plan loop
subtitle: Think roles and constraints first — dish name last.
chain: role → transformation → time filter → balance check → assemble

steps.title: Start with each ingredient
steps.body: Don’t open with a finished dish name. Ask what each item can become on this clock.

steps.title: What roles does the plate need?
steps.body: Every competitive plate needs coverage: something with body, something with contrast, brightness/acid, and (when the course fits) a clear main. Sauce or glaze is optional but often earns points.

steps.title: What roles can each ingredient play?
steps.body: The same ingredient can be main, accent, or texture depending on cut and technique — assign on purpose, not by habit.

steps.title: Pick the transformation
steps.body: For each role, name what must happen: sear, roast, reduce, smash + crisp, emulsify, etc. Use pick a lane below when you need concrete options.

steps.title: Apply the time filter
steps.body: Delete moves that don’t fit the round minutes and your equipment. Thick cuts, long braises, and unbounded reductions often fail here — shrink the cut, run parallel work, or change method.

steps.title: Combine what’s left
steps.body: The remaining moves are your valid set — stack them into one coherent plate story.

steps.title: Balance check
steps.body: Contrast (e.g. soft + crisp), sweet + acid where needed, depth from browning or a real reduction — then plate.

roles: Protein or main (when the course fits)
takeaway: Winners think in roles and constraints first, then name the dish — not the other way around.
